steps.title: Find the radius from the coordinates
steps.content: Point $A$ lies on the circle with center $C(h,k)$, so $CA$ is a radius. Use the distance from $C(h,k)$ to $A(h+2,k+\sqrt{113})$ to find that radius.

steps.title: Find the radius of the circle
steps.content:
Since point $A$ lies on the circle with center $C(h, k)$, the radius equals the distance from $C$ to $A$.
$$r = CA = \sqrt{(h+2-h)^2 + (k+\sqrt{113}-k)^2} = \sqrt{2^2 + (\sqrt{113})^2} = \sqrt{4 + 113} = \sqrt{117}$$

steps.title: Apply the Pythagorean theorem to the right triangle
steps.content:
Point $B$ also lies on the circle, so $CB = \sqrt{117}$ (the same radius). Since $\angle ACB$ is a right angle, triangle $ACB$ is a right triangle with legs $CA$ and $CB$.

By the Pythagorean theorem:
$$AB^2 = (\sqrt{117})^2 + (\sqrt{117})^2 = 234$$
$$AB = \sqrt{234}$$
That matches Choice A — $\sqrt{234}$.
